documents.title: SQL Injection Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '{user_id}'"

Secure code:
cur.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
$sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '" . $_POST['email'] . "'";

Secure code:
$stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?"); $stmt->execute([$_POST['email']]);

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 3 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
const sql = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${name}'`;

Secure code:
db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?', [name]);

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
String q = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id='" + id + "'";

Secure code:
PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?");

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 5 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='" + email + "'")

Secure code:
db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?", email)

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 6 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
User.where("email = '#{email}'")

Secure code:
User.where(email: email)

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
var sql = $"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Id = '{id}'";

Secure code:
var cmd = new SqlCommand("SELECT * FROM Users WHERE Id = @id", conn);

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 8 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
let q = format!("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '{}'", user_id);

Secure code:
sqlx::query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?").bind(user_id);

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 9 (perl)
documents.content:
Language: perl
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
$dbh->do("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '$name'");

Secure code:
$sth = $dbh->prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?'); $sth->execute($name);

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: SQL Injection Example 10 (scala)
documents.content:
Language: scala
Vulnerability: SQL Injection
Insecure code:
val q = s"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '$id'"

Secure code:
sql"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $id".as(...)

Why insecure:
Direct string interpolation in SQL lets attacker-controlled input alter query structure. Use parameterized statements and least-privilege database accounts.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 1 (javascript)
documents.content:
Language: javascript
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
el.innerHTML = userComment;

Secure code:
el.textContent = userComment;

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
echo $_GET['name'];

Secure code:
echo htmlspecialchars($_GET['name'], ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8');

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 3 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting Insecure code: return f'
{comment}
' Secure code: return render_template('view.html', comment=comment) Why insecure: Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
out.println(request.getParameter("q"));

Secure code:
out.println(StringEscapeUtils.escapeHtml4(request.getParameter("q")));

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 5 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
<%= raw params[:message] %>

Secure code:
<%= params[:message] %>

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 6 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
fmt.Fprintf(w, userInput)

Secure code:
template.HTMLEscape(w, []byte(userInput))

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting
Insecure code:
@Html.Raw(Model.Comment)

Secure code:
@Model.Comment

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 8 (vue)
documents.content:
Language: vue Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting Insecure code:
Secure code:
{{ message }}
Why insecure: Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 9 (react)

Language: react Vulnerability: Cross-Site Scripting Insecure code:
Secure code:
{text}
Why insecure: Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Cross-Site Scripting Example 10 (django)

Language: django

Insecure code:

Secure code:

Why insecure:
Rendering untrusted content as HTML or script allows arbitrary browser-side code execution. Prefer contextual output encoding and safe DOM APIs.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
os.system('ping ' + host)

Secure code:
subprocess.run(['ping', host], check=True)

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
system('nslookup ' . $_GET['host']);

Secure code:
$host = escapeshellarg($_GET['host']); system("nslookup $host");

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 3 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
exec('ping ' + host)

Secure code:
execFile('ping', [host])

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 4 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
system("ping #{host}")

Secure code:
system('ping', host)

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 5 (perl)
documents.content:
Language: perl
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
system("ping $host")

Secure code:
system('ping', $host)

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 6 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
exec.Command("sh", "-c", "ping "+host).Run()

Secure code:
exec.Command("ping", host).Run()

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 7 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
Runtime.getRuntime().exec("ping " + host);

Secure code:
new ProcessBuilder("ping", host).start();

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 8 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
Process.Start("cmd.exe", "/c ping " + host);

Secure code:
Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo("ping", host));

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 9 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
Command::new("sh").arg("-c").arg(format!("ping {}", host)).status();

Secure code:
Command::new("ping").arg(host).status();

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Command Injection Example 10 (bash)
documents.content:
Language: bash
Vulnerability: Command Injection
Insecure code:
eval "ping $HOST"

Secure code:
ping -- "$HOST"

Why insecure:
Concatenating user input into shell commands can execute arbitrary OS commands. Avoid shells and pass arguments as separate values.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
open('/var/data/' + filename)

Secure code:
open((BASE_DIR / Path(filename).name))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
readfile('/srv/files/' . $_GET['file']);

Secure code:
$name = basename($_GET['file']); readfile('/srv/files/' . $name);

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 3 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
fs.readFileSync('/srv/files/' + file)

Secure code:
fs.readFileSync(path.join(BASE, path.basename(file)))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
Paths.get(base + file)

Secure code:
Paths.get(base).resolve(Paths.get(file).getFileName())

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 5 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
os.ReadFile("/srv/files/" + name)

Secure code:
os.ReadFile(filepath.Join(base, filepath.Base(name)))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 6 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
File.read('/srv/files/' + params[:f])

Secure code:
File.read(File.join(BASE, File.basename(params[:f])))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
File.ReadAllText(baseDir + file)

Secure code:
File.ReadAllText(Path.Combine(baseDir, Path.GetFileName(file)))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 8 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
fs::read_to_string(format!("/srv/files/{}", file))

Secure code:
fs::read_to_string(base.join(Path::new(file).file_name().unwrap()))

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 9 (perl)
documents.content:
Language: perl
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
open my $fh, '<', "/srv/files/$file";

Secure code:
use File::Basename; open my $fh, '<', '/srv/files/' . basename($file);

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Path Traversal Example 10 (scala)
documents.content:
Language: scala
Vulnerability: Path Traversal
Insecure code:
Source.fromFile(base + name)

Secure code:
Source.fromFile(Paths.get(base, Paths.get(name).getFileName.toString).toFile)

Why insecure:
Joining untrusted file names directly into paths can expose arbitrary filesystem locations. Restrict to approved directories and normalize names.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
API_KEY = 'prod-secret-key'

Secure code:
API_KEY = os.environ['API_KEY']

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 2 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
const token = 'ghp_example';

Secure code:
const token = process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN;

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 3 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
$dbPass = 'supersecret';

Secure code:
$dbPass = getenv('DB_PASSWORD');

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
String secret = "aws-secret";

Secure code:

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 5 (go)

Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:

Secure code:

documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
SECRET = 'app-secret'

Secure code:

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
var key = "secret";

Secure code:
var key = Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("APP_KEY");

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 8 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:
let key = "secret";

Secure code:
let key = std::env::var("APP_KEY")?;

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.content:
Language: bash
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:

Secure code:

Why insecure:
Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: Hardcoded Secrets Example 10 (kotlin)
documents.content:
Language: kotlin
Vulnerability: Hardcoded Secrets
Insecure code:

Secure code:

Hardcoded credentials leak through repos, logs, and builds. Read secrets from environment variables or a dedicated secret manager.

documents.title: CSRF Example 1 (django)
documents.content:
Language: django
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
<form method='post'>...</form>

Secure code:
<form method='post'>{% csrf_token %}...</form>

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 2 (flask)
documents.content:
Language: flask
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
<form method='post'>...</form>

Secure code:
<form method='post'>{{ form.csrf_token }}...</form>

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 3 (rails)
documents.content:
Language: rails
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
<form action='/transfer' method='post'>

Secure code:
<%= form_with url: '/transfer' do |f| %>

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 4 (express)
documents.content:
Language: express
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
app.post('/pay', payHandler)

Secure code:
app.use(csrf()); app.post('/pay', payHandler)

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 5 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD']==='POST') transfer();

Secure code:
verify_csrf($_POST['csrf']); transfer();

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 6 (spring)
documents.content:
Language: spring
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:

Secure code:
http.csrf(Customizer.withDefaults());

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 7 (laravel)
documents.content:
Language: laravel
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
<form method='POST'>

Secure code:
<form method='POST'>@csrf

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 8 (aspnet)
documents.content:
Language: aspnet
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
<form method='post'>

Secure code:
<form method='post'>@Html.AntiForgeryToken()

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 9 (nextjs)
documents.content:
Language: nextjs
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
fetch('/api/update', {method:'POST'})

Secure code:
fetch('/api/update', {method:'POST', headers:{'X-CSRF-Token': token}})

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: CSRF Example 10 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: CSRF
Insecure code:
http.HandleFunc('/save', save)

Secure code:
csrf.Protect(key)(mux)

Why insecure:
State-changing requests without CSRF defenses can be triggered from attacker-controlled sites. Use CSRF tokens and cookie protections.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
return redirect(request.args['next'])

Secure code:
return redirect(validate_relative_path(request.args.get('next')))

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
header('Location: ' . $_GET['next']);

Secure code:
header('Location: ' . safe_redirect($_GET['next']));

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 3 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
res.redirect(req.query.next)

Secure code:
res.redirect(validateRelativePath(req.query.next))

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
response.sendRedirect(request.getParameter("next"));

Secure code:
response.sendRedirect(safeRelativePath(request.getParameter("next")));

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 5 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
redirect_to params[:next]

Secure code:
redirect_to safe_relative_path(params[:next])

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 6 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
http.Redirect(w, r, r.URL.Query().Get("next"), 302)

Secure code:
http.Redirect(w, r, safeRelativePath(r.URL.Query().Get("next")), 302)

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
return Redirect(next);

Secure code:
return LocalRedirect(next);

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 8 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
Redirect::to(next)

Secure code:
Redirect::to(validate_relative(next))

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 9 (kotlin)
documents.content:
Language: kotlin
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
return "redirect:" + next

Secure code:
return "redirect:" + safeRelativePath(next)

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Open Redirect Example 10 (scala)
documents.content:
Language: scala
Vulnerability: Open Redirect
Insecure code:
Redirect(next)

Secure code:
Redirect(safeRelative(next))

Why insecure:
Redirecting to unvalidated destinations enables phishing and token leakage. Restrict redirects to local relative paths or allowlisted hosts.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()

Secure code:
hashed = argon2_hasher.hash(password)

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
$hash = md5($password);

Secure code:
$hash = password_hash($password, PASSWORD_ARGON2ID);

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 3 (node)
documents.content:
Language: node
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
const hash = crypto.createHash('sha1').update(password).digest('hex')

Secure code:
const hash = await argon2.hash(password)

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 4 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
String hash = DigestUtils.md5Hex(password);

Secure code:
String hash = BCrypt.hashpw(password, BCrypt.gensalt());

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 5 (go)
documents.content:
Language: go
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
sum := md5.Sum([]byte(password))

Secure code:
hash, _ := bcrypt.GenerateFromPassword([]byte(password), bcrypt.DefaultCost)

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 6 (ruby)
documents.content:
Language: ruby
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
Digest::SHA1.hexdigest(password)

Secure code:
BCrypt::Password.create(password)

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 7 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
var hash = SHA1.HashData(bytes);

Secure code:
var hash = BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(password);

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 8 (rust)
documents.content:
Language: rust
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
let hash = format!("{:x}", md5::compute(password));

Secure code:
let hash = argon2::hash_encoded(password.as_bytes(), salt, &config)?;

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 9 (perl)
documents.content:
Language: perl
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
$hash = md5_hex($password);

Secure code:
$hash = Authen::Passphrase::BlowfishCrypt->new(cost => 12, salt_random => 1, passphrase => $password);

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: Weak Password Storage Example 10 (kotlin)
documents.content:
Language: kotlin
Vulnerability: Weak Password Storage
Insecure code:
val hash = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5")

Secure code:
val hash = BCrypt.hashpw(password, BCrypt.gensalt())

Why insecure:
Fast unsalted hashes are inadequate for password storage. Use adaptive password hashing such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt.

documents.title: XML External Entity Example 1 (java)
documents.content:
Language: java
Vulnerability: XML External Entity
Insecure code:
factory.newDocumentBuilder().parse(input)

Secure code:
factory.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true)

Why insecure:
Unsafe XML parsers can fetch external entities and expose files or internal network resources. Disable DTDs and external entity resolution.

documents.title: XML External Entity Example 2 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: XML External Entity
Insecure code:
xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring(data)

Secure code:
defusedxml.ElementTree.fromstring(data)

Why insecure:
Unsafe XML parsers can fetch external entities and expose files or internal network resources. Disable DTDs and external entity resolution.

documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: XML External Entity
Insecure code:
$dom->loadXML($xml);

Secure code:
$dom->loadXML($xml, LIBXML_NONET);

Why insecure:
Unsafe XML parsers can fetch external entities and expose files or internal network resources. Disable DTDs and external entity resolution.

documents.title: XML External Entity Example 4 (csharp)
documents.content:
Language: csharp
Vulnerability: XML External Entity
Insecure code:

Secure code:
var settings = new XmlReaderSettings { DtdProcessing = DtdProcessing.Prohibit };

Why insecure:
Unsafe XML parsers can fetch external entities and expose files or internal network resources. Disable DTDs and external entity resolution.

decoder := xml.NewDecoder(r); decoder.Strict = true

Unsafe XML parsers can fetch external entities and expose files or internal network resources. Disable DTDs and external entity resolution.

documents.title: Insecure Deserialization Example 1 (python)
documents.content:
Language: python
Vulnerability: Insecure Deserialization
Insecure code:
obj = pickle.loads(data)

Secure code:
obj = json.loads(data)

Why insecure:
Unsafe object deserialization can trigger gadget chains and code execution. Prefer simple data formats and validate structure strictly.

documents.title: Insecure Deserialization Example 2 (php)
documents.content:
Language: php
Vulnerability: Insecure Deserialization
Insecure code:
$obj = unserialize($_POST['data']);

Secure code:
$obj = json_decode($_POST['data'], true, flags: JSON_THROW_ON_ERROR);

Why insecure:
Unsafe object deserialization can trigger gadget chains and code execution. Prefer simple data formats and validate structure strictly.

documents.content:

new ObjectInputStream(in).readObject()

Why insecure:
Unsafe object deserialization can trigger gadget chains and code execution. Prefer simple data formats and validate structure strictly.
